title: The Capital Picture Hiding in Your EOFY Balance Sheet
excerpt: Most business owners open their June 30 accounts for the tax outcome and set them aside. The balance sheet contains something more immediately useful: a complete map of your capital position heading into the new financial year.
content:
Every June 30, a balance sheet is prepared. For most owner-led businesses, it gets reviewed alongside the profit and loss statement, a conversation is had with the accountant about the tax position, and the accounts are filed. The conversation tends to centre on what happened last year: what revenue landed, where deductions applied, whether the result was what was expected.

That conversation is worth having. But the balance sheet sitting in front of you is doing something the profit and loss statement cannot: it shows the capital position of the business at a single point in time, with complete clarity. What the business owns, what it owes, and what the equity base looks like on that day. That picture contains the answers to questions most owners find themselves asking in the months that follow: can we borrow more, should we reduce debt before we grow, do we have the working capital to pursue the opportunity we are looking at.

Reading the balance sheet for capital tells you where you stand before the new year's first decision needs to be made. With June 30 eleven days away, the accounts being prepared right now are the most complete capital structure snapshot the business will produce all year. Three reads are worth doing before they are filed away.

What the Working Capital Position Is Telling You

Working capital is the difference between current assets (cash, receivables, and inventory) and current liabilities (accounts payable, current loan instalments, and any ATO obligations due within twelve months). A business with more current assets than current liabilities has positive working capital: it can meet its short-term obligations from what it already holds. A business where current liabilities exceed current assets has a working capital deficit, meaning it relies on future revenue or additional financing to cover obligations that are already in the system.

The current ratio, current assets divided by current liabilities, puts a number on this position. A current ratio below 1.0 means the business has more obligations coming due in the next twelve months than liquid assets to meet them. This does not necessarily signal a crisis, but it does mean future cash inflows are being relied on to cover present commitments, which compresses the margin for unexpected timing variations in revenue or collections.

ATO liabilities sitting in current liabilities are worth examining separately. An ATO debt on a payment plan appears as a current liability and directly reduces the working capital position that any lender reads. Addressing ATO obligations before June 30, or confirming a formal plan with documented repayment dates, improves both the balance sheet picture and any bank conversation that follows in the new year.

The Net Debt Picture and What It Means for Borrowing Capacity

Net debt is total interest-bearing liabilities minus cash. For a business with $850,000 in bank loans and term facilities, and $180,000 sitting across its accounts, net debt is approximately $670,000. Divided by annual EBITDA, this ratio is what most commercial lenders use to assess how much additional borrowing capacity the business carries. Most banks are comfortable extending facilities where net debt sits below 2.5 times EBITDA. Above 3.0 times, unsecured debt typically becomes difficult to obtain without additional security, regardless of current trading performance.

Director loan balances are a common item that owners underestimate in this calculation. A loan owed by the business to a director sits in liabilities and increases net debt directly. A loan owed by a director to the business affects asset quality. Both positions influence what a lender sees when they run the numbers, and neither typically comes up in the tax conversation that EOFY accounts usually prompt. Understanding these balances before approaching a lender avoids a conversation that stalls on figures the owner was not prepared to discuss.

A

>Banking and Facility Review looks at the full debt structure, existing covenants, facility pricing against current market rates, and the net debt position against updated EOFY earnings, producing a refinancing recommendation where one is warranted. For businesses that have not reviewed their banking arrangements in the past two or three years, the review typically identifies either rate reductions or facility restructuring options.

The Equity Story the Balance Sheet Is Building Year on Year

Retained earnings are the cumulative profit the business has generated and left inside the entity rather than distributing. They appear in the equity section of the balance sheet and represent the owner's stake in the business net of what has been drawn. Over multiple years, a growing retained earnings balance signals two things to any lender or investor who reads it: that the business generates consistent profit, and that the owner manages finances with enough discipline to leave capital inside the structure.

A business that distributes the entirety of its profit each year carries thin equity on the balance sheet. This is not problematic from a trading perspective, but it does limit what a lender will extend without requiring property security or personal guarantees. The lender has no equity cushion to lean against beyond the business's own asset base, which in a service business is often modest.

For owners thinking about the financial year ahead, the equity position at June 30 connects directly to what capital instruments are available and on what terms. A business with a strong equity base and a clean net debt picture can typically raise debt at better pricing and with fewer covenants than one whose balance sheet shows sustained full distributions and limited retained capital. Understanding that picture before approaching a lender is the difference between arriving with leverage and arriving to be educated.

A

>capital raise preparation guide covers what lenders and investors assess in detail. For businesses where the capital picture at June 30 suggests the new year's growth plans may need external funding, the window between now and the end of July, when fresh EOFY accounts are available and no urgency has built, is when that preparation is most productive.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across

>Queensland and the East Coast to read the balance sheet as a capital planning tool, connecting what June 30 shows to the decisions available in the year ahead. If the EOFY accounts are almost ready and the capital conversation they contain has not yet been had, building that picture before the accounts are filed is worth the time it takes. faqs.q: What is working capital and how do I calculate it for my Australian business?
faqs.a: Working capital is the difference between your current assets (cash, accounts receivable, inventory) and your current liabilities (accounts payable, current loan repayments, and ATO obligations due within twelve months). Dividing current assets by current liabilities gives your current ratio. A ratio above 1.2 is generally considered healthy. A ratio below 1.0 means short-term obligations exceed liquid assets, which is a signal worth examining before starting the new financial year with growth ambitions.

faqs.q: What does a current ratio below 1.0 mean for my business?
faqs.a: A current ratio below 1.0 means the business has more obligations coming due in the next twelve months than it has in liquid assets to meet them. For a business with predictable and timely cash inflows, this can be manageable. For a business with variable collection timing or concentrated customer exposure, it introduces working capital risk. Lenders read a current ratio below 1.0 as a pressure signal and typically require additional security or co-signatories before extending new facilities in that position.

faqs.q: What is net debt to EBITDA and why do banks use it when assessing loans?
faqs.a: Net debt to EBITDA compares your total interest-bearing debt (minus cash) against your annual earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation. Banks use it to measure leverage: how many years of operating earnings would be needed to repay all outstanding debt. Most commercial lenders are comfortable extending additional facilities where this ratio is below 2.5. Above 3.0, unsecured lending typically becomes difficult to secure regardless of the current trading result. Knowing your ratio before approaching a lender prevents a conversation that stalls on figures you were not expecting.

faqs.q: How does an ATO payment plan affect my business balance sheet and bank applications?
faqs.a: An ATO debt on a payment plan appears as a current liability on your balance sheet. This reduces your working capital ratio and is included in the liabilities a commercial lender assesses when calculating your borrowing capacity. It does not automatically disqualify a business from new facilities, but the lender will factor it into their risk assessment. Addressing the ATO debt or documenting a formal repayment schedule before any bank conversation gives the lender a clearer and more complete picture.

faqs.q: How do retained earnings on my balance sheet affect my ability to borrow?
faqs.a: Retained earnings are the cumulative profit left inside the business rather than distributed. They sit in the equity section of your balance sheet and represent the owner's stake net of drawings. Lenders treat a growing retained earnings balance as a signal of both profitability and financial discipline. A business that retains a portion of profit consistently can typically borrow at better pricing and with fewer security requirements than one whose balance sheet shows thin equity from full distributions. An understanding of cash flow discipline underpins most of these balance sheet outcomes.

faqs.q: What is a banking and facility review and when should my business get one?
faqs.a: A banking and facility review examines your full debt structure, including overdraft facilities, term loans, equipment finance, and any director or related-party loans. It checks facility pricing against current market rates, reviews covenant conditions, and produces a refinancing recommendation where one is warranted. Most reviews identify rate reductions or restructuring options and are designed to pay for themselves through interest savings in the first year. The post-EOFY period is the natural time to commission one, when the accounts are fresh and any refinancing conversation is supported by a complete year of financial data. See ProfitPulse's services for more detail.

faqs.q: How do I know if my business is ready to raise capital in Australia in FY27?
faqs.a: The EOFY balance sheet gives you the starting picture. A current ratio above 1.2, a net debt to EBITDA below 2.5, and a growing retained earnings base are all positive signals. Any ATO liabilities, director loan balances, or thin equity need to be understood and addressed before approaching lenders or investors. A capital raise preparation guide outlines what financial positions lenders and investors look for and what documentation supports a well-structured approach.
